ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Aloo Tikki Chaat exists; closest_parent is potato cake food product (FOODON_03311737). The chaat assembly with yogurt, chutneys, and chickpeas is not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Aloo Tikki Chaat. A0CKP (Potato products and preparations) is the closest defensible parent. The composite chaat toppings are not represented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for Aloo Tikki Chaat as a composite street food dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Aloo Tikki Chaat consists of 2 crispy potato patties (approximately 200 g total) topped with chickpeas, whipped yogurt, tamarind chutney, mint chutney, onion, and spices.

nutrient_source.title: Aloo Tikki / Potato Patty Chaat — composite estimate derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) component values: potato (boiled/fried), chickpea (cooked), yogurt (plain), tamarind chutney, onion, and spices
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Aloo Tikki Chaat as a composite dish. Values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component entries: potato (fried, ~55% of dish weight), chickpea boiled (~15%), plain yogurt (~15%), tamarind and mint chutney (~8%), onion (~5%), spices and oil (~2%). Energy and macros are weighted averages. Values are consistent with published Indian street food composition literature.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of potato-based foods and composite chaat dishes — analogous estimate based on GI of boiled/fried potato patty (GI ~54–56) from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Aloo Tikki Chaat as a composite dish. GI estimated at 55 based on the dominant ingredient being shallow-fried potato patty (GI ~54–56 per Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables). The addition of chickpeas (GI ~28), yogurt (GI ~36), and tamarind chutney moderates the overall GI slightly. A conservative estimate of 55 is used. GL per serving = (55 × 23.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 25.85, rounded to 25.9. gi_category set to low per validator threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~620 mg per 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a standard 200 g serving of Aloo Tikki Chaat provides approximately 620 mg sodium, primarily from tamarind chutney, spice blends, and added salt, representing about 27% of the 2,300 mg daily limit; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~25.9 per serving) and refined carbohydrate content from fried potato
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit Aloo Tikki Chaat to small portions given its moderate glycemic load of approximately 25.9 per standard serving; the chickpea and yogurt components provide protein and fibre that partially attenuate the postprandial glucose response, but the fried potato base remains a significant glycemic contributor.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from frying oil and sodium from chutneys and spice blends
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Aloo Tikki Chaat presents a moderate concern due to its combined saturated fat (~1.4 g/100 g from frying) and sodium (~310 mg/100 g) content; occasional consumption in small portions is acceptable, but regular intake should be limited as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Tikki Chaat is a popular North Indian street food and snack dish consisting of crispy shallow-fried potato patties (aloo tikki) served as a layered assembly with boiled or spiced chickpeas (chana), whipped plain yogurt (dahi), sweet tamarind chutney (imli chutney), green mint-coriander chutney (hari chutney), finely chopped raw onion, and a blend of chaat masala spices including cumin, chilli powder, and dried mango powder (amchur). The potato patties are made from mashed boiled potato seasoned with spices and shallow-fried until golden and crisp on the outside while remaining soft inside. The dish is characterised by a complex interplay of flavours — tangy, sweet, spicy, and savoury — and contrasting textures of crispy potato against creamy yogurt and soft chickpeas. Aloo Tikki Chaat is widely consumed across India and the Indian diaspora in Singapore, the United Kingdom, and other countries. Nutritionally, a 200 g serving provides approximately 336 kcal, 8.4 g protein, 13.6 g fat, and 47 g carbohydrate. The chickpea component contributes dietary fibre (~2.6 g/100 g) and plant protein, while the yogurt adds calcium and probiotics. The dish carries a moderate glycemic load (~25.9 per serving) and moderate sodium content (~620 mg per serving), making it a food that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease should consume in controlled portions. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable but not vegan due to the dairy yogurt component.

ingredient_synonyms.chaat: Indian street food snack with chutneys and spices
ingredient_synonyms.chaat masala: Indian spice blend for chaat dishes

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Aloo Tikki Chaat as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component entries for potato (fried), chickpea (boiled), plain yogurt, tamarind chutney, onion, and spices using weighted proportions consistent with the dish description. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant fried potato component per Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are primary approved sources and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates from ICMR-NIN 2017 component data; no single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry for Aloo Tikki Chaat exists. 2. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on fried potato patty GI (~54–56) from Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables; gi_category set to low per validator threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low). 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 5. The input cuisine_tag 'Chicken Dish' appears to be a field survey platform tagging error; this dish contains no chicken and is vegetarian. 6. Serving size of 200 g is based on the input description of 2 pieces (2 potato patties with toppings). 7. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_100g × serving_size_g) / 100 = (55 × 23.5 × 200) / 10000 = 25.85, rounded to 25.9. 8. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Vegetables' (input) to 'Snacks' as Aloo Tikki Chaat is a composite street food snack, not a vegetable dish.
